paragraphs.text:
SRD-46 shows that the **strongest Pb²⁺ binders in this lead subset are not the standard clinical antidotes**; they are chiefly **aza-macrocycles** and **thiol ligands**.

| Ligand / class | SRD-46 status for Pb²⁺ | Clinical use note |
|---|---:|---|
| **[36]aneN12** (ligand_7541) | Very strong Pb²⁺ binder; 12 Pb²⁺ measurements at 25 °C, I = 0.1 M | Not a standard clinical chelator |
| **[33]aneN11** (ligand_7540) | Very strong Pb²⁺ binder; 11 Pb²⁺ measurements at 25 °C, I = 0.1 M | Not a standard clinical chelator |
| **D-penicillamine** (ligand_5857) | Strongest **clinically relevant** ligand found in this extract; 13 Pb²⁺ volumes / 7 β values, 25–37 °C, I = 0.1–3 M | **Yes** — clinically used for heavy-metal chelation, but not the main modern first-line agent for lead |
| **Cysteine** (ligand_5856) | Strong sulfur-donor Pb²⁺ ligand | Not used clinically as lead chelation therapy |
| **Glutathione** (ligand_6592) | Strong sulfur-donor Pb²⁺ ligand | Not a standard clinical antidote |
| **Citric acid / citrate** (ligand_9058) | Lower-affinity O-donor ligand | Not a primary clinical lead chelator |
| **Glycine** (ligand_5760) | Lower-affinity ligand | Not clinical for lead poisoning |
| **Succinic acid / succinate** (ligand_8907) | Lower-affinity ligand | Not clinical for lead poisoning |

paragraphs.text:
### Bottom line
| Question | Answer |
|---|---|
| **Which ligands in SRD-46 appear to have the highest affinity for Pb²⁺?** | The top binders in the retrieved Pb²⁺ set are **aza-macrocycles** such as **[36]aneN12** and **[33]aneN11**, followed by strong **sulfur-donor ligands** such as **D-penicillamine**, cysteine, and glutathione. |
| **Are any of them used clinically?** | **Yes: D-penicillamine** is the clearest clinically used chelator found in this SRD-46 lead subset. |
| **What about EDTA, DMSA/succimer, DMPS, or BAL?** | In this session, those names did **not resolve as ligand records** in SRD-46 using the available name searches, so I cannot claim SRD-46 support for them here. The Pb²⁺ extract also explicitly noted that **succimer (DMSA) was not present** in the retrieved lead-ligand set. |

claims.text: ### Bottom line
Which ligands in SRD-46 appear to have the highest affinity for Pb²⁺?
The top binders in the retrieved Pb²⁺ set are **aza-macrocycles**
followed by strong **sulfur-donor ligands**
such as **D-penicillamine**, cysteine, and glutathione
Are any of them used clinically?
**Yes: D-penicillamine** is the clearest clinically used chelator found in this SRD-46 lead subset.
What about EDTA, DMSA/succimer, DMPS, or BAL?
In this session, those names did **not resolve as ligand records** in SRD-46 using the available name searches
so I cannot claim SRD-46 support for them here.
The Pb²⁺ extract also explicitly noted that
**succimer (DMSA) was not present** in the retrieved lead-ligand set.

paragraphs.text:
### Important interpretation note
| Caveat | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Constant definitions vary (L, HL, H₂L, H₃L bases) | Reported log β/log K values are **not directly comparable** unless they refer to the same reaction stoichiometry and protonation basis |
| Conditions differ | Temperature and ionic strength affect measured affinity |
| “Highest affinity” does not mean “best drug” | Clinical usefulness also depends on toxicity, selectivity, oral availability, and ability to form excretable complexes |

Chemically, the pattern makes sense: **Pb²⁺ is a soft/borderline Lewis acid**, so **sulfur donors** often bind more strongly than simple oxygen-donor ligands like citrate. The very high-affinity **polyaza macrocycles** can outperform clinical agents in thermodynamic binding, but that does **not** make them suitable therapeutically.

**Source:** NIST SRD-46.

| Conditions differ | Temperature and ionic strength affect measured affinity |
| “Highest affinity” does not mean “best drug” | Clinical usefulness also depends on toxicity, selectivity, oral availability, and ability to form excretable complexes |
Chemically, the pattern makes sense:
**Pb²⁺ is a soft/borderline Lewis acid**
**sulfur donors** often bind more strongly than simple oxygen-donor ligands like citrate.
The very high-affinity **polyaza macrocycles** can outperform clinical agents in thermodynamic binding,
but that does **not** make them suitable therapeutically.
**Source:** NIST SRD-46.
